title: Frontend Deployment
content:
Objective In this section, we will deploy the frontend of the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform project.
The frontend of this project is built with Next.js and deployed as a static website. After the build process, the static files will be uploaded to the Amazon S3 Frontend Bucket and distributed to the Internet through Amazon CloudFront.
The main contents of this section include:
Configure environment variables for the frontend. Build the Next.js frontend. Upload static files to Amazon S3. Create a CloudFront invalidation. Check the website after deployment. Test the main customer and admin features. Frontend architecture The frontend is deployed using a static hosting model combined with a CDN.
Frontend access flow:
User / Admin ↓ Amazon CloudFront ↓ Amazon S3 Frontend Bucket ↓ Next.js static files When users access the website, the request goes through CloudFront. CloudFront retrieves content from the S3 Frontend Bucket and returns it to the user.
When the frontend needs to get product data, log in, place orders, or manage admin features, it calls API Gateway.
Frontend-to-backend flow:
Frontend Website ↓ Amazon API Gateway ↓ AWS Lambda ↓ Amazon DynamoDB Move to the frontend folder Open the terminal at the project directory:
C:\Users\namda\ecommerce-aws Then move to the frontend folder:
cd frontend Check the main files in the frontend folder:
frontend/ ├── src/ ├── public/ ├── package.json ├── next.config.ts ├── tsconfig.json └── .env.local Where:
src/ contains the Next.js UI source code. public/ contains images and static assets. package.json contains scripts and dependencies. next.config.ts contains Next.js configuration. tsconfig.json contains TypeScript configuration. .env.local contains environment variables used by the frontend. Install frontend dependencies Inside the frontend folder, run:
npm install This command installs the required libraries to run and build the frontend.
After installation, check that the following folder has been created:
frontend/node_modules/ Configure frontend environment variables The frontend needs backend information such as the API Gateway URL, Cognito User Pool ID, and Cognito App Client ID.
Create or open the following file:
frontend/.env.local Add the following content:
NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL=https://89yap0gq5k.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod NEXT_PUBLIC_AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-1 NEXT_PUBLIC_CUSTOMER_USER_POOL_ID=ap-southeast-1_LHSScZri7 NEXT_PUBLIC_CUSTOMER_USER_POOL_CLIENT_ID=1uijbf07brda1p130293v3prfv NEXT_PUBLIC_ADMIN_USER_POOL_ID=ap-southeast-1_k57hCsM4z NEXT_PUBLIC_ADMIN_USER_POOL_CLIENT_ID=1qc3ttu4q3e95pk7mlqsivr66e NEXT_PUBLIC_PRODUCT_IMAGES_BASE_URL=https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/uploads Notes:
With Next.js, environment variables used in the browser must start with NEXT_PUBLIC_. Do not put real secrets in the .env.local file. These values are taken from the Outputs tab of the CloudFormation stack. If API Gateway or Cognito changes, update the .env.local file and rebuild the frontend. Check package.json Open the following file:
frontend/package.json Check that the scripts section contains the main commands as follows:
{ "scripts": { "dev": "next dev", "build": "next build", "start": "next start", "lint": "next lint" } } In this workshop, we will use:
npm run build to build the production frontend.
Run the frontend locally for testing Before deployment, you should run the frontend locally to test the UI and API connection.
Run the command:
npm run dev Expected result:
Local: http://localhost:3000 Open the browser:
http://localhost:3000 Check the main pages:
Home page. Product list page. Product detail page. Cart page. Checkout page. Customer login page. Customer registration page. Order history page. Admin login page. Admin dashboard. Product management page. Order management page. Refund management page. Build the production frontend After local testing is successful, build the production frontend:
npm run build Expected result:
Compiled successfully If the project is configured for static export, Next.js will create an output folder after the build process. This folder will be uploaded to S3.
The output folder is usually:
frontend/out/ Handle common build errors TypeScript error in the .next folder If you encounter an error related to:
.next/dev/types/validator.ts it may be caused by a broken Next.js type cache.
How to fix:
rm -rf .next npm run build On Windows PowerShell, you can use:
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force .next npm run build Missing environment variables If the frontend calls the wrong API or does not display data, check the following file again:
frontend/.env.local Make sure the environment variables have the following prefix:
NEXT_PUBLIC_ API CORS error If the browser shows a CORS error when the frontend calls API Gateway, check the CORS configuration in API Gateway or Lambda response headers.
Common required headers include:
Access-Control-Allow-Origin Access-Control-Allow-Headers Access-Control-Allow-Methods Check the output folder After the build succeeds, check the following folder:
frontend/out/ This folder contains static files such as:
out/ ├── index.html ├── products/ ├── account/ ├── admin/ ├── _next/ └── 404.html These files will be uploaded to the S3 Frontend Bucket.
Upload frontend to Amazon S3 After the build is complete, upload all contents inside the out folder to the S3 Frontend Bucket.
Upload command:
aws s3 sync out/ s3://ecommerce-aws-backend-frontendbucket-9wu7aid8okd8 --delete Explanation:
out/ is the folder containing static files after the build. s3://ecommerce-aws-backend-frontendbucket-9wu7aid8okd8 is the Frontend Bucket. --delete removes old files from S3 if they no longer exist locally. Check files in the S3 bucket Open AWS Console:
Amazon S3 → Buckets Select the Frontend Bucket:
ecommerce-aws-backend-frontendbucket-9wu7aid8okd8 Check whether the frontend files have been uploaded, for example:
index.html 404.html _next/ products/ admin/ account/ If these files appear in the bucket, the frontend upload process has completed successfully.
Create CloudFront invalidation After uploading the frontend to S3, create a CloudFront invalidation so CloudFront can fetch the latest version.
Run the command:
aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id E3EAY8XN54SSDX --paths "/*" Explanation:
E3EAY8XN54SSDX is the CloudFront Distribution ID. --paths "/*" asks CloudFront to refresh the entire cache. The result will look like this:
{ "Location": "https://cloudfront.amazonaws.com/...", "Invalidation": { "Id": "IXXXXXXXXXXXX", "Status": "InProgress" } } Check CloudFront invalidation Open AWS Console:
CloudFront → Distributions Select the distribution:
E3EAY8XN54SSDX Open the tab:
Invalidations Expected status:
Completed If the status is still:
InProgress wait a few more minutes.
Open the website using the CloudFront URL After the invalidation is completed, open the website using the CloudFront URL:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net If the website displays the correct UI, the frontend has been deployed successfully.
Check customer pages Access and check the customer pages:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/ https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/products https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/product https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/cart https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/checkout https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/account/login https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/account/register https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/account/orders Features to check:
The home page is displayed correctly. The product list is loaded from the API. The product detail page works. Products can be added to the cart. The checkout page is displayed correctly. Customers can register. Customers can log in. Customers can view order history. Check admin pages Access and check the admin pages:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/login https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/products https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/orders https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/refunds Features to check:
Admin can log in. Admin dashboard is displayed correctly. Admin can view the product list. Admin can create a new product. Admin can upload product images. Admin can view orders. Admin can manage refunds. Check frontend requests to API Gateway Open Developer Tools in the browser:
F12 → Network Then reload the website and check API requests.
Common API requests include:
/products /product /cart /checkout /account/orders /admin/products /admin/orders If the status code is:
200 it means the frontend successfully called the API.
If you see:
401 Unauthorized check the login token or Cognito configuration.
If you see:
403 Forbidden check permissions or the authorizer.
If you see:
500 Internal Server Error open CloudWatch Logs of the Lambda function to debug.
Common errors when deploying frontend Website still shows the old version The common cause is that CloudFront is still caching the old version.
How to fix:
aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id E3EAY8XN54SSDX --paths "/*" Child routes fail when refreshing For example, refreshing this page:
/admin/products returns a 403 or 404 error.
The cause is that S3 does not understand Next.js static export routes automatically.
How to fix:
Check the CloudFront Function or error response rewrite. Make sure CloudFront rewrites routes to index.html or the correct HTML file. Check the Next.js static export configuration. Frontend calls the wrong API URL If the frontend calls the wrong backend URL, check:
frontend/.env.local After editing .env.local, rebuild the frontend:
npm run build Then upload it to S3 again.
Product images are not displayed Check the following possible causes:
The product image URL in DynamoDB is correct. The S3 Product Images Bucket contains the image file. CloudFront distributes the correct /uploads path. S3 CORS is configured correctly. Admin is logged out or session expired This may happen because the Cognito token has expired or the User Pool Client configuration is incorrect.
How to fix:
Log in again as admin. Check the Admin User Pool ID. Check the Admin User Pool Client ID. Check frontend environment variables. Result After completing this section, you have successfully deployed the frontend of the e-commerce project to AWS.
The results include:
The Next.js frontend has been built into static files. Static files have been uploaded to the Amazon S3 Frontend Bucket. CloudFront has distributed the website to the Internet. CloudFront invalidation has been created to update the latest version. Customers can access the shopping website. Admin can access the admin dashboard. The frontend can call API Gateway. The frontend has connected to Cognito for customer and admin login. The website is ready for testing product, cart, order, and payment features. In the next section, we will configure and test authentication using Amazon Cognito for customers and admins.

title: Authentication with Amazon Cognito
content:
Objective In this section, we will configure and test user authentication for the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform project using Amazon Cognito.
The system uses Amazon Cognito to manage registration, login, and access control for two main user groups:
Customer: users who purchase products on the website. Admin: system administrators who manage products, orders, and refunds. The main contents of this section include:
Check that Cognito User Pools have been created. Configure Cognito information for the frontend. Test customer account registration. Test customer account confirmation. Test customer login. Test admin login. Check authentication tokens. Check API Gateway Authorizer. Handle common authentication errors. Authentication architecture The system uses Amazon Cognito to authenticate users before allowing access to protected APIs.
Customer login flow:
Customer ↓ Frontend Login Page ↓ Amazon Cognito Customer User Pool ↓ JWT Token ↓ Frontend stores token ↓ Call protected API Gateway endpoints Admin login flow:
Admin ↓ Admin Login Page ↓ Amazon Cognito Admin User Pool ↓ JWT Token ↓ Frontend stores token ↓ Call admin API Gateway endpoints Authenticated API request flow:
Frontend ↓ Authorization: Bearer JWT Token ↓ Amazon API Gateway Authorizer ↓ AWS Lambda ↓ Amazon DynamoDB Cognito User Pools in the project The project uses two separate Cognito User Pools:
Customer User Pool: used for customers. Admin User Pool: used for administrators. Separating the two User Pools makes access control and security easier to manage.
User Pool information in this workshop:
Customer User Pool ID: ap-southeast-1_LHSScZri7 Customer User Pool Client ID: 1uijbf07brda1p130293v3prfv Admin User Pool ID: ap-southeast-1_k57hCsM4z Admin User Pool Client ID: 1qc3ttu4q3e95pk7mlqsivr66e Check Cognito User Pools in AWS Console Open AWS Console and go to:
Amazon Cognito → User pools Check the list of User Pools.
You should see the User Pools related to the e-commerce project, for example:
Customer User Pool Admin User Pool Check Customer User Pool Select the Customer User Pool.
Check the following information:
User pool ID: ap-southeast-1_LHSScZri7 Then open the following section:
App integration → App clients Check the App Client ID:
1uijbf07brda1p130293v3prfv The Customer User Pool is used for:
Customer account registration. Account confirmation. Customer login. Viewing order history. Viewing order details. Cancelling orders if the system allows it. Check Admin User Pool Go back to the User Pools list and select the Admin User Pool.
Check the User Pool ID:
ap-southeast-1_k57hCsM4z Check the App Client ID:
1qc3ttu4q3e95pk7mlqsivr66e The Admin User Pool is used for:
Admin login. Accessing the admin dashboard. Managing products. Uploading product images. Viewing order lists. Viewing order details. Managing refunds. Configure frontend environment variables The frontend needs environment variables to connect to Cognito.
Open the file:
frontend/.env.local Check the following variables:
NEXT_PUBLIC_AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-1 NEXT_PUBLIC_CUSTOMER_USER_POOL_ID=ap-southeast-1_LHSScZri7 NEXT_PUBLIC_CUSTOMER_USER_POOL_CLIENT_ID=1uijbf07brda1p130293v3prfv NEXT_PUBLIC_ADMIN_USER_POOL_ID=ap-southeast-1_k57hCsM4z NEXT_PUBLIC_ADMIN_USER_POOL_CLIENT_ID=1qc3ttu4q3e95pk7mlqsivr66e Notes:
With Next.js, environment variables used in the browser must start with NEXT_PUBLIC_. Do not store secrets in the frontend .env.local file. If the User Pool or App Client ID changes, rebuild the frontend and upload it to S3 again. Run frontend locally to test authentication Move to the frontend folder:
cd frontend Run the frontend locally:
npm run dev Open the browser:
http://localhost:3000 Authentication pages to test:
http://localhost:3000/account/register http://localhost:3000/account/login http://localhost:3000/account/confirm http://localhost:3000/admin/login Test customer registration Go to the customer registration page:
http://localhost:3000/account/register Enter a new account, for example:
Email: customer-test@example.com Password: Test@123456 Name: Test Customer Then click the register button.
If registration is successful, the system will create a user in the Customer User Pool.
Check customer user in Cognito After registration, open AWS Console:
Amazon Cognito → User pools → Customer User Pool → Users Check that the new user has been created.
The user status may be:
UNCONFIRMED or:
CONFIRMED If Cognito requires email confirmation, the user must enter the confirmation code or click the confirmation link before logging in.
Test customer account confirmation If the system requires account confirmation, open the email used for registration.
Find the confirmation email from AWS Cognito or from the system.
Then enter the confirmation code on this page:
http://localhost:3000/account/confirm Example:
Email: customer-test@example.com Confirmation Code: 123456 After successful confirmation, the user status in Cognito will change to:
CONFIRMED Test customer login Go to the customer login page:
http://localhost:3000/account/login Enter the registered email and password:
Email: customer-test@example.com Password: Test@123456 After successful login, the frontend will receive tokens from Cognito.
The customer can access pages that require login, such as:
/account /account/orders /account/order-detail Check customer token in the browser Open Developer Tools:
F12 → Application Check storage areas such as:
Local Storage Session Storage Cookies Depending on how the frontend stores tokens, you may see information such as:
idToken accessToken refreshToken This token will be sent with requests to APIs that require authentication.
A common request header looks like this:
Authorization: Bearer <JWT_TOKEN> Test customer protected API After the customer logs in, try opening the order history page:
http://localhost:3000/account/orders Open Developer Tools:
F12 → Network Check API requests such as:
/account/orders /account/order-detail If the request returns:
200 it means the token is valid and API Gateway Authorizer has authenticated it successfully.
If the request returns:
401 Unauthorized it means the token is missing, expired, or invalid.
Create or check admin account Admins should usually not register themselves like customers. Admin accounts should be created directly in the Admin User Pool.
Open AWS Console:
Amazon Cognito → User pools → Admin User Pool → Users Click:
Create user Enter an admin email, for example:
admin@example.com Set a temporary password or permanent password depending on the configuration.
After creation, the admin user will appear in the Admin User Pool.
Test admin login Go to the admin login page:
http://localhost:3000/admin/login Enter the admin account:
Email: admin@example.com Password: Admin@123456 After successful login, admin can access:
/admin /admin/products /admin/orders /admin/refunds Check admin protected API calls After admin logs in, open:
http://localhost:3000/admin/products Open Developer Tools:
F12 → Network Check admin APIs:
/admin/products /admin/orders /admin/order-detail /admin/refunds /admin/uploads/product-image If the API returns:
200 it means the admin token is valid.
If the API returns:
401 Unauthorized it means the token is missing or expired.
If the API returns:
403 Forbidden it means the user is logged in but does not have permission to access that endpoint, or the authorizer is using the wrong User Pool.
API Gateway Authorizer In the backend, API Gateway uses Cognito JWT Authorizer to protect endpoints that require login.
Public endpoints usually do not require a token:
/products /product /cart /checkout /payments/zalopay/callback Customer endpoints require a customer token:
/account/register /account/login /account/orders /account/order-detail /account/cancel-order Admin endpoints require an admin token:
/admin/products /admin/orders /admin/order-detail /admin/refunds /admin/uploads/product-image API Gateway authentication flow:
Frontend sends request ↓ Authorization header contains JWT token ↓ API Gateway validates token with Cognito User Pool ↓ If token is valid, request is forwarded to Lambda ↓ Lambda processes business logic Check Authorizer in AWS Console Open AWS Console:
API Gateway → APIs Select the API of the e-commerce project.
Check the section:
Authorization / Authorizers You should see an authorizer related to Cognito or JWT.
Check the following:
Customer authorizer uses the correct Customer User Pool. Admin authorizer uses the correct Admin User Pool. Customer routes are attached to the customer authorizer. Admin routes are attached to the admin authorizer. Public routes do not require an authorizer. Check CloudWatch Logs when login fails If login or API calls fail, open CloudWatch Logs:
CloudWatch → Logs → Log groups Select the log group of the backend Lambda function.
Check the latest log stream to see the error.
Some information to check:
Request path HTTP method Authorization header User identity Error message Status code Common authentication errors User is not confirmed If the customer cannot log in, check the user status in Cognito.
If the user status is:
UNCONFIRMED the account must be confirmed first.
How to fix:
Check the confirmation email. Enter the confirmation code on the confirmation page. Or manually confirm the user in Cognito when testing. Wrong User Pool ID or Client ID If the frontend cannot log in or shows a configuration error, check the file:
frontend/.env.local Make sure the following variables are correct:
NEXT_PUBLIC_CUSTOMER_USER_POOL_ID NEXT_PUBLIC_CUSTOMER_USER_POOL_CLIENT_ID NEXT_PUBLIC_ADMIN_USER_POOL_ID NEXT_PUBLIC_ADMIN_USER_POOL_CLIENT_ID After editing, rebuild the frontend:
npm run build Token expired If the website has been used for a while and the API returns:
401 Unauthorized the token may have expired.
How to fix:
Log in again. Check the refresh token flow if the system supports it. Clear the old token in browser storage and log in again. Using customer token for admin endpoint If a customer token is used to call an admin endpoint, the API may return:
401 Unauthorized or:
403 Forbidden How to fix:
Log out as customer. Log in using an admin account. Check that the frontend uses the correct admin token when calling /admin/*. Admin has not changed temporary password If the admin was created with a temporary password, Cognito may require a password change during the first login.
How to fix:
Log in as admin. Complete the password change step if required. Or reset the password in Cognito Console when testing. API route is not attached to the correct authorizer If a protected endpoint can still be accessed without login, the route may not be attached to an authorizer.
How to fix:
Check template.yaml. Check the route in API Gateway. Make sure customer routes use the customer authorizer. Make sure admin routes use the admin authorizer. Redeploy the backend after making changes. Test authentication on CloudFront URL After local testing is successful, test again on the deployed website:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/account/register https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/account/login https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/login Features to check:
Customers can register. Customers can confirm their account. Customers can log in. Customers can view order history. Admin can log in. Admin can access the admin dashboard. Users who are not logged in cannot access pages that require authentication. Tokens are sent correctly in API requests. Result After completing this section, the system has authentication functionality using Amazon Cognito.
The results include:
Customer User Pool has been checked. Admin User Pool has been checked. Frontend has been configured with Cognito User Pool ID and App Client ID. Customers can register accounts. Customers can confirm accounts. Customers can log in. Admin can log in. Frontend can store and send JWT tokens. API Gateway Authorizer can validate tokens from Cognito. Customer endpoints are protected. Admin endpoints are protected. The system is ready to test product management, order placement, and payment features. In the next section, we will test product management features, including creating products, updating products, and uploading product images to Amazon S3.

title: Workshop
content:
Introduction to Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Project Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Project is an online shopping website built using the serverless model on AWS. The project focuses on deploying an e-commerce application with essential features such as product browsing, login, cart management, order placement, online payment, order management, and product image upload.
The project architecture uses AWS managed services and serverless services to reduce manual server management. Instead of deploying the system on EC2 or managing servers manually, the project uses Amazon S3, Amazon CloudFront, Amazon Cognito, Amazon API Gateway, AWS Lambda, Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon EventBridge, Amazon CloudWatch, Amazon SNS, AWS IAM, AWS SAM, and AWS CloudFormation.
The project also integrates ZaloPay Sandbox to simulate the online payment process. In addition, the system includes background tasks such as checking expired unpaid orders, refund reconciliation, and sending alerts through CloudWatch Alarm combined with SNS.
The main objective of this project is to build an e-commerce system that can be deployed on AWS, is scalable, easy to operate, includes monitoring, supports CI/CD, and follows a production-like architecture.
Content Workshop overview Prerequiste Backend deployment Frontend deployment Authentication Product management Clean up

title: Product Management
content:
Objective In this section, we will test the product management feature of the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform project.
The product management feature is used by admins to create products, update products, view the product list, and upload product images to Amazon S3.
The main contents of this section include:
Check the product management page in the admin dashboard. Check the product management API. Create a new product. Upload product images to Amazon S3. Check product data in DynamoDB. Check product images in the S3 Product Images Bucket. Check whether products are displayed on the customer page. Handle common errors when managing products. Product management architecture The product management feature uses multiple AWS services together.
Admin product creation flow:
Admin ↓ Admin Product Page ↓ Amazon API Gateway ↓ AWS Lambda ↓ Amazon DynamoDB Products Table Admin product image upload flow:
Admin ↓ Admin Product Page ↓ API Gateway ↓ Lambda creates presigned URL ↓ Upload image to Amazon S3 Product Images Bucket ↓ Save image URL to DynamoDB Customer product viewing flow:
Customer ↓ Frontend Product Page ↓ Amazon API Gateway ↓ AWS Lambda ↓ Amazon DynamoDB Products Table Product images are stored in Amazon S3 and distributed through CloudFront.
Product Images Bucket ↓ Amazon CloudFront ↓ Customer Browser AWS resources used The product management feature uses the following main resources:
Amazon Cognito: authenticates admin users. Amazon API Gateway: receives requests from the frontend. AWS Lambda: handles product creation, update, and retrieval logic. Amazon DynamoDB: stores product data. Amazon S3: stores product images. Amazon CloudFront: distributes images and the website. Amazon CloudWatch Logs: checks logs when errors occur. Resource information in this workshop:
Products Table: EcommerceProducts Product Images Bucket: ecommerce-aws-backend-productimagesbucket-eqn2cddpurel API Gateway URL: https://89yap0gq5k.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod CloudFront URL: https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net Check that admin is logged in Before managing products, the admin must log in successfully.
Access the admin login page:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/login Log in using the admin account created in the Admin User Pool.
After logging in successfully, access the product management page:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/products If the admin is not logged in or the token has expired, the system may redirect to the login page or return the following error:
401 Unauthorized Open the product management page Access the following page:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/products This page allows admin users to:
View the product list. Create a new product. Update product information. Upload product images. Check inventory. Check product status. Check frontend API call for product list Open Developer Tools in the browser:
F12 → Network Reload the admin products page and check the API request.
Common endpoint:
/admin/products If the request returns:
200 it means the frontend called the API successfully and the admin token is valid.
If the request returns:
401 Unauthorized it means the token is missing, expired, or invalid.
If the request returns:
403 Forbidden it means the account does not have permission to access the admin API or the authorizer is configured incorrectly.
Create a new product On the admin products page, click the button to create a new product.
Enter product information, for example:
Product Name: Silver Heart Necklace Category: Necklace Description: A simple and elegant silver heart necklace for daily wear. Price: 490000 Stock: 25 Status: Active Then click the save button.
When the admin creates a product, the frontend sends a request to the backend through API Gateway.
Processing flow:
Admin Product Form ↓ POST /admin/products ↓ API Gateway ↓ Lambda ↓ DynamoDB Products Table Check product creation request In Developer Tools, open the following tab:
Network Check the product creation request.
Endpoint:
POST /admin/products Sample payload:
{ "name": "Silver Heart Necklace", "category": "Necklace", "description": "A simple and elegant silver heart necklace for daily wear.", "price": 490000, "stock": 25, "status": "ACTIVE" } Expected response:
{ "success": true, "message": "Product created successfully" } If the API returns a successful response, the new product will be saved to DynamoDB.
Check product in DynamoDB Open AWS Console:
DynamoDB → Tables Select the table:
EcommerceProducts Open:
Explore table items Check the product that was just created.
A product item usually contains fields such as:
productId name category description price stock status imageUrl createdAt updatedAt Check presigned URL request Open Developer Tools:
F12 → Network When uploading an image, check the following request:
POST /admin/uploads/product-image Sample payload:
{ "fileName": "silver-heart-necklace.png", "contentType": "image/png" } Expected response:
{ "uploadUrl": "https://...", "imageUrl": "https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/uploads/..." } Where:
uploadUrl is the presigned URL used to upload the image directly to S3. imageUrl is the public URL used to display the product image on the website. Check image in S3 Product Images Bucket Open AWS Console:
Amazon S3 → Buckets Select the bucket:
ecommerce-aws-backend-productimagesbucket-eqn2cddpurel Check the folder or prefix:
uploads/ After the upload succeeds, you will see the product image file in the bucket.
Example:
uploads/silver-heart-necklace.png Update product information Admin can update product information such as:
Product name. Product description. Category. Price. Stock quantity. Product status. Product image. Example update:
Product Name: Silver Heart Necklace Price: 450000 Stock: 30 Status: Active When updating a product, the frontend will call the API:
PUT /admin/products or the corresponding update endpoint in the backend.
Check data after update After updating the product, check again:
DynamoDB → Tables → EcommerceProducts → Explore table items Make sure the following fields have been updated correctly:
price stock status updatedAt imageUrl If the data in DynamoDB has changed, it means the backend updated the product successfully.
Check product on customer page After creating and updating the product, open the customer product page:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/products Check whether the product you created is displayed.
Information to check:
Product name. Product image. Product price. Product category. Display status. View detail button or add to cart button. Check product detail page Select the product you created to open the product detail page.
The URL may look like this:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/product?id=PRODUCT_ID or another route used by the frontend.
Check the following information:
Product name. Product description. Price. Image. Stock. Add to cart button. If the information is displayed correctly, it means the public product detail API is working properly.
Check public product API You can check the public API using a browser or Postman.
Product list endpoint:
https://89yap0gq5k.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod/products Product detail endpoint:
https://89yap0gq5k.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod/product If the API returns the product list or product details, it means the backend has successfully read data from DynamoDB.
Sample response:
[ { "productId": "product-001", "name": "Silver Heart Necklace", "category": "Necklace", "price": 450000, "stock": 30, "status": "ACTIVE", "imageUrl": "https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/uploads/silver-heart-necklace.png" } ] Check CloudWatch Logs when API errors occur If product creation, image upload, or product listing fails, open CloudWatch Logs:
CloudWatch → Logs → Log groups Select the log group of the backend Lambda function.
Check the latest logs.
Information to check:
Request path HTTP method Request body Authorization header Product ID Error message Status code Common errors when managing products Admin is not logged in If an admin API is called without login, the API will return:
401 Unauthorized How to fix:
Log in again using the admin account. Check the token in browser storage. Check whether the request contains the Authorization header. Using the wrong customer token If a customer token is used to call an admin API, the API may return:
403 Forbidden How to fix:
Log out of the customer account. Log in again using the admin account. Check that the frontend is using the correct admin token. Image upload fails If image upload fails, check:
Whether the /admin/uploads/product-image API returns a presigned URL. Whether the image file format is valid. Whether the S3 bucket exists. Whether the Lambda IAM Role has permission to create a presigned URL. Whether S3 CORS is configured correctly. Image uploaded successfully but not displayed If the image exists in S3 but is not displayed on the website, check:
Whether imageUrl has been saved to DynamoDB. Whether the CloudFront URL is correct. Whether CloudFront distributes the /uploads path correctly. Whether the image file has the correct content type. Whether the browser is caching an old image. Product is not displayed on the customer page If the product was created but is not displayed on the customer page, check:
Whether the product status is ACTIVE. Whether the /products API returns the product. Whether DynamoDB contains the product data. Whether the frontend calls the correct API base URL. Whether the browser console shows any JavaScript errors. DynamoDB write error If the API returns an error when creating or updating a product, check:
Whether the Lambda IAM Role has permission to write to the EcommerceProducts table. Whether the table name in the Lambda environment variables is correct. Whether the API payload contains all required fields. Whether CloudWatch Logs show validation or permission errors. CORS error when calling API If the browser shows a CORS error when calling the API, check:
Whether API Gateway has CORS enabled. Whether the Lambda response returns CORS headers. Whether the frontend origin is allowed. Common required headers:
Access-Control-Allow-Origin Access-Control-Allow-Headers Access-Control-Allow-Methods Test product management on CloudFront URL After local testing is successful, test again on the deployed website:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/admin/products https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net/products Features to check:
Admin can view the product list. Admin can create a new product. Admin can update product information. Admin can upload product images. Images are stored in the S3 Product Images Bucket. Products are stored in DynamoDB. Customers can view products on the products page. Customers can open the product detail page. Result After completing this section, the system has product management functionality for admins.
The results include:
Admin can access the product management page. Admin can create new products. Admin can update product information. Admin can upload product images to Amazon S3. Product image URLs are saved in DynamoDB. Products are stored in the EcommerceProducts table. Customers can view the product list. Customers can view product details. Public APIs /products and /product are working. Admin APIs /admin/products and /admin/uploads/product-image are working. The system is ready to test cart, order, and payment features. In the next section, we will test the order and payment flow, including adding products to the cart, checkout, creating orders, and processing payment through ZaloPay Sandbox.

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at AWS-FCAJ from April 17, 2026 to July 10, 2026, I had the opportunity to learn, practice, and apply the knowledge I gained at university in a real working environment.
I participated in the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Project, through which I improved many important skills such as frontend development, backend development, AWS cloud deployment, requirement analysis, debugging, technical documentation, teamwork, and progress reporting.
Regarding my working attitude, I always tried to complete assigned tasks properly, follow company rules, and actively communicate with colleagues to improve work efficiency.
Skills gained during the project Through the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform project, I learned and practiced the following skills:
Frontend development skills: building an e-commerce website interface using Next.js, including product pages, cart, checkout, customer login, and admin dashboard. Backend development skills: building APIs for products, orders, payments, customer accounts, and admin functions using Node.js/TypeScript on AWS Lambda. AWS Serverless skills: using services such as AWS Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, CloudFront, Cognito, EventBridge, CloudWatch, SNS, and SES. Infrastructure as Code skills: using AWS SAM and CloudFormation to define, build, and deploy backend resources to AWS. Database management skills: designing and using DynamoDB to store products, orders, payment status, and refund information. Authentication and authorization skills: using Amazon Cognito to separate customer and admin login, and protecting APIs with JWT Authorizer. File upload handling skills: creating presigned URLs to upload product images to Amazon S3 and distributing images through CloudFront. Payment integration skills: integrating ZaloPay Sandbox for checkout, creating payment URLs, handling payment callbacks, and updating order status. Monitoring and debugging skills: using CloudWatch Logs to debug Lambda functions, API Gateway requests, payment callbacks, and scheduled jobs. System alerting skills: configuring CloudWatch Alarms and SNS to receive email alerts when errors or abnormal metrics occur. Basic CI/CD skills: understanding the deployment process for frontend and backend, and using GitHub/GitHub Actions during development. Basic AWS security skills: understanding IAM Roles, Policies, CORS, JWT tokens, and access control for cloud resources. Functional testing skills: testing main flows such as registration, login, product management, ordering, payment, and order management. Technical documentation skills: creating workshop content, describing architecture, deployment steps, screenshots, and cleanup instructions. Teamwork and communication skills: communicating with mentors, receiving feedback, reporting progress, and collaborating to complete project tasks. Improve discipline: I need to follow working hours, company rules, and working procedures more strictly in any organization. Improve problem-solving thinking: I need to practice analyzing root causes, breaking down problems, and finding solutions in a more systematic way. Improve communication skills: I need to present ideas more clearly, communicate actively when facing difficulties, and respond better during teamwork. Improve time management: I need to organize tasks by priority and avoid leaving important work until close to the deadline. Improve English technical reading skills: Since many AWS and framework documents are written in English, I need to read and understand technical documents faster and more accurately. Improve debugging skills: I need to improve my ability to read logs, identify errors, and check systems in a more structured way. Improve knowledge of system security: I need to learn more about IAM, API authorization, user data protection, and best practices for deploying applications on the cloud. Improve report writing skills: I need to present content more clearly, use suitable screenshots, and describe the project implementation process more effectively. Be more proactive in asking questions and receiving feedback: I need to communicate more confidently with mentors or colleagues when facing issues to save time and learn faster. Continue improving technical knowledge: I need to study backend, frontend, cloud deployment, CI/CD, and system design more deeply so that I can contribute better to real projects. Overall, the internship helped me better understand the real working environment, the process of deploying a cloud-based e-commerce project, and the skills required of a software developer. This experience is an important foundation for me to continue improving myself and developing my career path in software engineering and cloud computing.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
During my participation in the First Cloud AI Journey program at AWS-FCAJ, I had the opportunity to learn, practice, and experience a real working environment through the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform project. This was a very valuable experience for me, helping me better understand the process of building an e-commerce system on AWS, from application development, infrastructure deployment, and functional testing to monitoring and resource cleanup.
Through this internship, I would like to share some of my feedback and personal thoughts as follows:
General Evaluation 1. Working environment
The working environment at AWS-FCAJ was friendly, open, and suitable for interns. During my participation, I felt that I had many opportunities to learn new knowledge, especially knowledge related to AWS Cloud, serverless architecture, and the process of deploying a real project.
The working environment encouraged me to proactively research, experiment, and solve issues by myself during the project. This helped me not only learn theory but also better understand how to apply knowledge in practice.
However, I think that having more experience-sharing sessions among interns or with previous participants would help everyone learn from each other more effectively.
2. Support from mentor and admin team
The mentor and admin team supported me a lot during the internship. When I encountered difficulties while working on the project, I was guided on how to analyze problems, identify errors, and come up with solutions instead of simply receiving direct answers.
This helped me improve my self-learning ability, problem-solving mindset, and debugging skills. In particular, while working on the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform project, I learned how to check errors through CloudWatch Logs, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, Cognito, and other AWS services.
I highly appreciate the mentor’s practical guidance, which helped me understand why each step was necessary instead of only following commands.
3. Relevance between assigned work and my major
The internship work was highly related to my major in Software Engineering. The project required knowledge of frontend development, backend development, databases, authentication, APIs, deployment, and cloud computing.
Through the project, I was able to apply the knowledge I had learned at university, such as web programming, API design, data management, and functional testing. In addition, I also had the opportunity to learn new areas that I had not practiced deeply at school, such as:
Deploying backend services with AWS Lambda and API Gateway.
Storing data with Amazon DynamoDB.
Managing user authentication with Amazon Cognito.
Uploading product images to Amazon S3.
Distributing the website with Amazon CloudFront.
Monitoring errors with Amazon CloudWatch.
Configuring alerts with SNS.
Integrating payment deeply at school, such as:
Deploying backend services with AWS Lambda and API Gateway.
Storing data with Amazon DynamoDB.
Managing user authentication with ZaloPay Sandbox.
Managing infrastructure with AWS SAM and CloudFormation.
As a result, I gained a clearer understanding of how a real web system is deployed and operated in a cloud environment.
4. Learning opportunities and skill development
During the internship, I learned many important skills that support my career goal of becoming a full-stack developer and cloud developer.
In terms of technical skills, I improved my frontend development skills with Next.js, built backend services with Node.js/TypeScript, designed APIs, handled authentication, managed data in DynamoDB, and deployed systems to AWS.
In terms of deployment skills, I learned how to use AWS SAM to build and deploy backend resources, configure CloudFormation stacks, check outputs, deploy frontend files to S3, and create CloudFront invalidations.
In terms of debugging skills, I learned how to read logs, inspect requests in Developer Tools, debug API errors such as 401, 403, and 500, check CORS issues, verify Cognito tokens, and inspect IAM permissions.
In addition, I also practiced technical documentation skills by building workshop content, describing architecture, recording deployment steps, adding screenshots, and writing the resource cleanup section.
5. Culture and teamwork spirit
I felt the spirit of support and sharing throughout the program. When I faced problems, I could discuss them and receive suitable suggestions or guidance.
The working process helped me understand that communication and collaboration are very important in a real project. A software developer not only needs to know how to code but also needs to report progress, present problems clearly, receive feedback, and improve the product based on suggestions.
I also learned that when errors occur, it is important to stay calm and check each part of the system step by step instead of fixing issues in a hurry. This is a very necessary skill in a real working environment.
6. Policies and support for interns
The program created good conditions for interns to learn and practice with new technologies. Having the opportunity to work with AWS and build a project with relatively complete features gave me a more practical view of the software industry.
I think this program is very suitable for students who want to learn more about cloud, backend, frontend, and the process of deploying real systems.
What I was most satisfied with during the internship What I was most satisfied with was being able to directly participate in building and deploying an e-commerce project on AWS from beginning to end.
Through the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform project, I not only learned how to write code but also understood more about system architecture, how AWS services connect with each other, and how to deploy a complete website.
I was especially impressed by the experience of deploying the frontend to S3 and CloudFront, deploying the backend to Lambda, storing data in DynamoDB, handling login with Cognito, and processing payments with ZaloPay Sandbox. These experiences are very useful for my future career direction.
What I think the program could improve In my opinion, the program could improve in the following areas:
Provide a weekly task checklist so interns can track their progress more easily. Organize more code review or architecture review sessions so students can better understand the strengths and weaknesses of their projects. These improvements would help interns have clearer direction, learn in a more structured way, and feel more confident when participating in real projects.
Would I recommend this internship to my friends? If I have friends who are interested in cloud computing, web development, backend, frontend, or AWS, I would recommend them to join this program.
The reason is that the program helps students experience a real working environment, practice on a specific project, and learn technologies that are widely used in businesses.
Especially for Software Engineering students, understanding cloud deployment, serverless architecture, CI/CD, monitoring, and basic security is very important. This knowledge can give students an advantage when applying for internships or jobs in the future.
Suggestions and expectations I hope the program will continue to be maintained and developed in the future so that more students can have the opportunity to learn and gain practical experience.
I also hope that in future programs, interns will have more opportunities to:
Join more in-depth workshops about AWS. Receive additional guidance on system design and cloud architecture. Practice more with CI/CD, security, and monitoring. Other feedback After the internship, I realized that I had learned many new things, not only about technical knowledge but also about working attitude, communication, and self-learning.
I understand that to become a good developer, besides knowing how to write code, I also need to improve many other skills such as problem-solving thinking, discipline, time management, English technical reading, report writing, and teamwork.
I would like to thank AWS-FCAJ, my mentor, and the admin team for giving me the opportunity to participate in this internship program. This was an important experience that gave me more motivation to continue learning, improving myself, and pursuing my career path in software engineering and cloud computing.

title: Cleanup
content:
Objective In this section, we will clean up the AWS resources created during the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform workshop.
Cleaning up resources after completing the workshop is important to avoid unnecessary AWS charges.
The main contents of this section include:
Review the resources created during the workshop. Back up important data if needed. Empty S3 buckets before deleting the stack. Delete the CloudFormation stack using AWS SAM. Check for remaining resources. Delete GitHub Actions OIDC resources if they are no longer used. Delete or disable related services such as SES, SNS, and CloudWatch Alarms. Check AWS Billing after cleanup. Resources to clean up In this workshop, the system created multiple AWS resources, including:
Amazon S3 Frontend Bucket. Amazon S3 Product Images Bucket. Amazon CloudFront Distribution. Amazon API Gateway HTTP API. AWS Lambda Functions. Amazon DynamoDB Tables. Amazon Cognito User Pools. Amazon EventBridge Schedules. Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Amazon CloudWatch Alarms. Amazon SNS Topic. Amazon SES Identity. IAM Roles and Policies. AWS CloudFormation Stack. Main stack information:
CloudFormation Stack Name: ecommerce-aws-backend AWS Region: ap-southeast-1 Notes before cleanup Cleanup will delete system resources and data.
Before continuing, check the following:
Do you need to keep product data? Do you need to keep order data? Do you need to keep product images? Do you need to keep Cognito users? Do you need to keep source code or configuration files? If you need to keep any data, back it up before deleting the stack.
Check CloudFormation stack Open AWS Console:
CloudFormation → Stacks Select the stack:
ecommerce-aws-backend Check the following tabs:
Resources Outputs Events The Resources tab shows which resources were created by the stack.
Back up DynamoDB data if needed If you want to keep product or order data, export or scan the data before deletion.
Main tables:
EcommerceProducts EcommerceOrders You can check the data in AWS Console:
DynamoDB → Tables → EcommerceProducts → Explore table items DynamoDB → Tables → EcommerceOrders → Explore table items If you do not need to keep the data, you can skip this step.
Check S3 buckets Open AWS Console:
Amazon S3 → Buckets Main buckets in this workshop:
ecommerce-aws-backend-frontendbucket-9wu7aid8okd8 ecommerce-aws-backend-productimagesbucket-eqn2cddpurel The frontend bucket stores static website files.
The product images bucket stores uploaded product images.
Empty S3 Frontend Bucket Before deleting the CloudFormation stack, you need to empty the S3 bucket because CloudFormation cannot delete a bucket that still contains objects.
Run the following command:
aws s3 rm s3://ecommerce-aws-backend-frontendbucket-9wu7aid8okd8 --recursive This command deletes all frontend files in the bucket.
After the command finishes, check the bucket again in AWS Console to make sure it is empty.
Empty S3 Product Images Bucket Next, delete all product images from the Product Images Bucket:
aws s3 rm s3://ecommerce-aws-backend-productimagesbucket-eqn2cddpurel --recursive After deleting the files, check the bucket again:
Amazon S3 → Buckets → Product Images Bucket The bucket should no longer contain any objects.
Delete object versions if bucket versioning is enabled If S3 bucket versioning is enabled, the aws s3 rm --recursive command may not delete all object versions.
In this case, open AWS Console:
Amazon S3 → Buckets → select bucket → Versions Then delete all object versions and delete markers.
You can also use the AWS Console option:
Empty bucket and confirm that all object versions should be deleted.
Delete backend stack using AWS SAM Move to the backend or infrastructure folder depending on your project structure:
cd C:\Users\namda\ecommerce-aws\backend Run the delete command:
sam delete When SAM asks for confirmation, enter:
y SAM will delete the CloudFormation stack and related resources.
Stack to delete:
ecommerce-aws-backend Delete stack using AWS CLI If you do not use sam delete, you can delete the stack using AWS CLI:
aws cloudformation delete-stack --stack-name ecommerce-aws-backend --region ap-southeast-1 Then monitor the deletion status:
aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name ecommerce-aws-backend --region ap-southeast-1 In AWS Console, the stack status will change to:
DELETE_IN_PROGRESS After the deletion is complete, the stack will disappear from the active stacks list.
Check CloudFormation Events if delete fails If stack deletion fails, open:
CloudFormation → Stacks → ecommerce-aws-backend → Events Check which resource caused the error.
Common causes include:
S3 bucket is not empty. S3 bucket still contains object versions. CloudFront Distribution has not finished disabling. IAM Role is still being used. Lambda function still has related event sources or permissions. If the error is related to S3, empty the bucket and delete the stack again.
Check CloudFront Distribution CloudFront Distribution may take a few minutes to be fully deleted.
Open AWS Console:
CloudFront → Distributions Check the distribution:
E3EAY8XN54SSDX If the distribution still exists, check its status:
Enabled Disabled In Progress In some cases, CloudFormation needs to disable the distribution first before it can delete it.
This process may take several minutes.
Check API Gateway After the stack is deleted, check API Gateway:
API Gateway → APIs The e-commerce project API should no longer appear.
If the API still exists, check whether it was created outside CloudFormation.
Workshop API URL:
https://89yap0gq5k.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod After cleanup, this URL should no longer work.
Check Lambda Functions Open AWS Console:
Lambda → Functions Check for Lambda functions related to the e-commerce project.
Function names usually contain:
ecommerce-aws-backend After deleting the stack, these functions should be removed.
If any function still exists, check whether it was created outside the stack.
Check DynamoDB Tables Open AWS Console:
DynamoDB → Tables Check the following tables:
EcommerceProducts EcommerceOrders If CloudFormation deletion was successful, these tables should no longer exist.
If the tables still exist, they may have a DeletionPolicy: Retain configuration or may have been created manually outside the stack.
Check Cognito User Pools Open AWS Console:
Amazon Cognito → User pools Check the Customer User Pool and Admin User Pool.
User Pools in this workshop:
ap-southeast-1_LHSScZri7 ap-southeast-1_k57hCsM4z After cleanup, User Pools created by the stack should be deleted.
If they still exist and are no longer used, you can delete them manually in Cognito Console.
Check EventBridge Schedules Open AWS Console:
Amazon EventBridge → Scheduler or:
Amazon EventBridge → Rules Check scheduled jobs related to:
payment expiry refund reconciliation ecommerce-aws-backend After deleting the stack, these schedules should also be deleted.
If any schedule still exists, delete it manually to avoid unwanted Lambda executions.
Check CloudWatch Logs CloudWatch Log Groups may not be deleted automatically depending on the configuration.
Open AWS Console:
CloudWatch → Logs → Log groups Search for log groups containing:
/aws/lambda/ecommerce-aws-backend If you do not need to keep the logs, you can delete these log groups manually.
Check CloudWatch Alarms Open AWS Console:
CloudWatch → Alarms Check alarms related to the e-commerce project.
After deleting the stack, alarms created by the stack should be removed.
If any alarm still exists, delete it manually to avoid unnecessary alerts.
Check SNS Topic Open AWS Console:
Amazon SNS → Topics Check the topic used to send alert emails.
If the topic still exists and is no longer used, delete it manually.
Then check:
Amazon SNS → Subscriptions Delete email subscriptions if they are no longer needed.
Check SES Identity If you verified an email or domain in Amazon SES, the identity may still exist after stack deletion.
Open AWS Console:
Amazon SES → Configuration → Identities Check the verified email or domain.
If you no longer use SES for this project, you can delete the identity.
Notes:
Deleting an SES identity means that email or domain can no longer be used to send emails through SES. If the email is used by another project, you should not delete it. Check Secrets Manager If the ZaloPay secret was created in AWS Secrets Manager, check whether it was created by the stack or manually.
Open AWS Console:
AWS Secrets Manager → Secrets Search for secrets related to:
zalopay ecommerce If the secret is no longer used, you can delete it.
When deleting a secret, AWS may require a waiting period before permanent deletion.
Check IAM Roles and Policies Open AWS Console:
IAM → Roles Search for roles related to:
ecommerce-aws-backend IAM Roles created by CloudFormation are usually deleted with the stack.
If roles or policies still exist, check carefully before deleting them manually.
Do not delete a role if it is being used by another project.
Check GitHub Actions OIDC If you created an OIDC Provider or IAM Role for GitHub Actions CI/CD, check it in IAM.
Open AWS Console:
IAM → Identity providers If the following provider exists:
token.actions.githubusercontent.com only delete it if it is not used by any other repository or project.
Check the IAM Role for GitHub Actions:
IAM → Roles Search for roles related to:
GitHubActions ecommerce OIDC If the role is used only for this project and is no longer needed, you can delete it.
Check GitHub Secrets In the GitHub repository, open:
Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions Check the following secrets:
AWS_REGION AWS_ROLE_TO_ASSUME ZALOPAY_APP_ID ZALOPAY_KEY1 ZALOPAY_KEY2 If the repository will no longer deploy to AWS, you can delete these secrets.
Check website after cleanup After cleanup is complete, open the CloudFront URL:
https://dfjng6e5jz4mf.cloudfront.net If the resources have been deleted, the website should no longer be accessible or should return an error.
Check the API URL:
https://89yap0gq5k.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod/products If the API has been deleted, this endpoint will no longer work.
Check Billing After deleting the resources, open AWS Billing:
AWS Billing and Cost Management → Bills Check services that may still generate costs:
CloudFront. S3. DynamoDB. Lambda. API Gateway. CloudWatch Logs. SNS. SES. Secrets Manager. WAF if used. Some services may still show charges for the current day because billing data can have a delay.
Common cleanup errors CloudFormation cannot delete S3 bucket Common error:
The bucket you tried to delete is not empty How to fix:
Empty the bucket. Delete object versions if versioning is enabled. Run sam delete or delete the stack again. CloudFront deletion takes a long time CloudFront needs time to disable and delete a distribution.
How to fix:
Wait a few minutes. Check the distribution status. Do not recreate a stack with conflicting resources while the old distribution is still being deleted. Stack is DELETE_FAILED If the stack status becomes:
DELETE_FAILED open the Events tab to see which resource failed.
After fixing the issue, choose:
Delete stack and try again.
Log groups still exist after stack deletion CloudWatch Log Groups sometimes remain to preserve historical logs.
How to fix:
Open CloudWatch Logs. Select log groups related to the project. Delete them manually if you do not need to keep the logs. DynamoDB table still exists If a DynamoDB table is not deleted, possible causes include:
DeletionPolicy: Retain is configured. The table was created manually outside the stack. Stack deletion failed before deleting the table. How to fix:
Check CloudFormation Events. Delete the table manually if you no longer need the data. Cleanup checklist After finishing cleanup, verify the following checklist:
S3 Frontend Bucket has been emptied or deleted. S3 Product Images Bucket has been emptied or deleted. CloudFormation stack ecommerce-aws-backend has been deleted. API Gateway no longer contains the project API. Lambda functions of the project have been deleted. DynamoDB tables have been deleted or backed up. Cognito User Pools have been deleted if no longer used. EventBridge schedules are no longer running. CloudWatch Alarms have been deleted. SNS Topic and Subscriptions have been deleted if no longer used. SES Identity has been deleted if not needed. Secrets Manager secrets have been deleted if not used. IAM Roles and Policies no longer contain unused project resources. GitHub Actions OIDC Role has been deleted if no longer used. GitHub Secrets have been deleted if no longer used. Billing no longer shows abnormal charges. Result After completing the cleanup section, the AWS resources for the workshop have been cleaned up.
The results include:
Frontend static files on S3 have been deleted. Product images on S3 have been deleted. The main CloudFormation stack has been deleted. Related resources such as API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, Cognito, CloudFront, EventBridge, CloudWatch, and SNS have been deleted or checked. Remaining resources such as SES, Secrets Manager, IAM Roles, and GitHub Actions OIDC have been checked. Billing has been checked to make sure there are no abnormal charges. After this step, the Mimi Jewelry E-commerce Platform workshop has completed the full deployment lifecycle: environment preparation, backend deployment, frontend deployment, authentication, product management, order and payment, monitoring, and cleanup.
